skill.name: analyzing-financial-statements
skill.description: Calculates and interprets financial ratios from income statement, balance sheet, cash flow, and market data.
skill.summary: Analyze company performance with financial ratios, benchmarks, trends, and concise interpretation.
skill.author: anthropics

security_audit.summary: AI review dismissed the static findings as false positives caused by financial terminology and Markdown filename formatting. The Python files perform local ratio calculation and interpretation only, with no network access, environment reads, subprocess execution, prompt injection text, or data exfiltration evidence found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Detector Matched Finance Text
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged lines describe financial metrics, analysis, text input, and benchmarking. They do not import cryptography modules, call weak algorithms, hash secrets, or perform encryption. Confidence: 0.98. Confidence reasoning: The reviewed locations are documentation strings or Markdown prose, and no cryptographic code is present in the referenced files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matched content is financial-analysis prose, not cryptographic implementation. No weak cryptographic primitive or security-sensitive cryptography use was found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Reconnaissance Detector Matched Financial Ratio Content
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged lines reference an acid-test quick ratio and financial monitoring recommendations. They do not inspect the host system, enumerate files, collect environment data, or execute reconnaissance commands. Confidence: 0.96. Confidence reasoning: The context is ordinary financial ratio logic and recommendation text with no operating-system interaction.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code uses financial terms such as quick ratio and monitor in business recommendations. No system discovery API or command usage is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Are Not Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. SKILL.md lines 53 and 54 use Markdown backticks to format script filenames. They are not Ruby or shell backtick execution and are not executable code. Confidence: 0.99. Confidence reasoning: The locations are bullet-list documentation entries naming local Python files, not runtime code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Backticks occur only in Markdown documentation. There is no shell, Ruby, subprocess, or command execution context at these lines.

content.value_statement: Financial statement review can be slow when ratios must be calculated across many line items. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code compute ratios, interpret results, and summarize company health.
actual_capabilities: Calculates profitability ratios including ROE, ROA, gross margin, operating margin, and net margin.
Calculates liquidity, leverage, efficiency, valuation, and per-share metrics from structured inputs.
Formats ratio values and generates short interpretations for common financial indicators.
Compares selected ratios against simplified industry benchmark ranges.
Analyzes ratio trends when multiple periods of historical values are provided.
Produces an overall financial health assessment and prioritized recommendations.
limitations: Requires accurate income statement, balance sheet, cash flow, and market data.
Uses simplified benchmark ranges that may not fit every company or industry.
Does not fetch market data, filings, or prices from external sources.
Does not provide investment, legal, tax, or accounting advice.

use_cases.title: Screen a Public Company
use_cases.description: Calculate core profitability, liquidity, leverage, and valuation ratios before deeper investment research.

use_cases.title: Prepare Credit Review Notes
use_cases.description: Summarize debt, coverage, liquidity, and trend signals for a borrower review memo.

use_cases.title: Teach Ratio Analysis
use_cases.description: Generate clear examples that show how common financial ratios are calculated and interpreted.

prompt_templates.title: Calculate Basic Ratios
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner analysis with supplied financial statement values.
prompt_templates.prompt: Calculate the main profitability, liquidity, leverage, efficiency, and valuation ratios from this financial data. Explain each result in simple terms.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Industry Benchmarks
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate analysis with a known industry sector.
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze these ratios for a company in the technology industry. Compare the results with available benchmarks and highlight strengths and concerns.

prompt_templates.title: Review Multi-Period Trends
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced analysis using historical ratio values.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the current ratios and the historical data to identify improving, stable, and deteriorating trends. Prioritize the most important changes.

prompt_templates.title: Create an Analyst Summary
prompt_templates.prompt: Prepare a concise financial analysis report with ratio calculations, benchmark interpretation, trend commentary, key risks, and recommended follow-up questions.

output_examples.input: Revenue is 1,000,000, net income is 150,000, assets are 2,000,000, equity is 1,500,000, current assets are 800,000, and current liabilities are 400,000.
output_examples.output: The company has a 15.0% net margin, 7.5% ROA, 10.0% ROE, and a 2.0 current ratio. Profitability is moderate, and liquidity appears strong based on these inputs.

output_examples.input: Analyze a retail company with current ratio 1.1, debt-to-equity 2.2, ROE 8%, gross margin 22%, and P/E 30.
output_examples.output: Liquidity is acceptable for retail, leverage is high, and ROE is below stronger retail benchmarks. The P/E ratio suggests valuation risk unless growth expectations are well supported.

output_examples.input: Current ratio changed from 1.8 to 1.4 to 1.1 over three years, while debt-to-equity rose from 0.6 to 1.0 to 1.5.
output_examples.output: The trend shows weakening liquidity and increasing leverage. Review working capital drivers, refinancing needs, and whether operating cash flow can support debt obligations.

best_practices: Provide complete financial statements with clear period labels and consistent units.
Specify the industry sector before asking for benchmark interpretation.
Use ratio results as screening evidence and verify conclusions with source filings.
anti_patterns: Do not treat simplified benchmark comparisons as a final investment decision.
Do not mix annual, quarterly, and trailing data without labeling each period.
Do not ignore one-time items that distort earnings, debt, or cash balances.

faq.question: What data does this skill need?
faq.answer: It works best with income statement, balance sheet, cash flow, and market data for the same period.

faq.question: Can it analyze trends?
faq.answer: Yes. Provide values and period labels for two or more periods to generate trend commentary.

faq.question: Does it download financial statements?
faq.answer: No. The skill uses data supplied by the user and does not fetch external filings or prices.

faq.question: Which industries are benchmarked?
faq.answer: It includes simplified benchmark ranges for technology, retail, financial, manufacturing, healthcare, and general analysis.

faq.question: Can this replace professional financial advice?
faq.answer: No. It supports analysis and education, but it is not investment, accounting, tax, or legal advice.

faq.question: Can Claude Code run the included scripts?
faq.answer: Yes. Claude Code can use the local Python scripts to calculate ratios from structured financial data.
